title: Chapter 32: Canceled
content: The phone wakes me up by burning through the nightstand.

Not vibrating. Burning. The thing is genuinely warm to the touch, like it's been running a crypto miner all night, and when I flip it over the lock screen is a waterfall of notifications that doesn't stop — it keeps loading, spinning, pulling down more and more like some kind of nightmare scroll that feeds on itself.
#ChloeHartLies.

Trending. Number three in the United States, right between a basketball trade and a Marvel trailer. My name, trending, with the word "lies" attached to it, and the phone is so hot against my palm that I have to shift it from hand to hand while I read.
Someone leaked the lawsuit.

The story is everywhere before I've even brushed my teeth. "Ungrateful influencer bites the hand that made her." That's the headline that keeps repeating, like someone copy-pasted it across a hundred different accounts at the same time — because someone did. That's how these things work. You don't trend organically. You trend by design.

And the DMs. Fabricated DMs, screenshots so obviously fake that any forensic analyst would laugh them out of court, but forensic analysts don't drive engagement, do they? The DMs show me — or rather, a version of me — complaining about my followers, calling them "livestock," talking about how easy it is to manipulate them. Stuff I would never say. Stuff I couldn't say, even if I wanted to. I literally don't have the capacity for that kind of contempt anymore. Isn't that ironic? They're accusing me of being a sociopath when the truth is I've been surgically prevented from being one.

Then there are the "insider accounts." Anonymous sources who worked with me. Stories about my "erratic behavior" — the emotional flatness, the times I seemed distant or robotic, the interviews where my affect was "off." They're describing the symptoms. They just don't know it.
The comments section is a crime scene.
"She seems so cold lately."
"She's changed."
"Used to be so real."
You have no idea how right you are.
I call Evelyn. She doesn't pick up. I text her: "Did you see?" She texts back six minutes later: "Don't engage. Don't post. Don't respond. We'll address it at the hearing."
The hearing. Tomorrow.

I call Leo. He picks up on the first ring, which means he's been awake. Probably been awake all night, monitoring. That's what he does — he watches the data streams like they're weather systems, and right now there's a Category 5 bearing down on my life.
"It's a coordinated campaign," he says. "The fingerprints are all over it. Bot networks, seeded narratives, the whole playbook. But here's the problem — it's not just bots."
"I know."
"Real journalists are picking it up. Not just internet randos. Actual press."
"I know, Leo."
The algorithm can't defend me this time.

That's the thing nobody tells you about being hollowed. The algorithm inside me — Mephisto's code, the thing that runs my content calendar and optimizes my engagement — it's designed to protect the brand. Chloe Hart, the product. But it's not designed to protect Chloe Hart, the plaintiff. It can't argue a legal case. It can't refute a press narrative. It can spin a trend but it can't cross-examine a witness.

And Mephisto knows this. He's not attacking me through other influencers this time. He's not sending drone accounts or orchestrating ratio wars. He's going through the legitimate press. The respectable press. The kind of press that judges read over their morning coffee.

The New York Post runs it front-page: INFLUENCER SUES MANAGEMENT CO., CLAIMS 'SOUL THEFT' — Experts say delusional. There's a photo of me from a red carpet last year, before I signed, when I could still smile with my eyes. The "expert" — some clinical psychologist they found who's never met me — says the claims are "consistent with a schizotypal presentation" and that "high-profile individuals often construct elaborate narratives to explain ordinary contractual dissatisfaction."

The Wall Street Journal is worse because it's calmer. UNPRECEDENTED IP CASE RAISES QUESTIONS ABOUT DIGITAL CONTRACTS. They don't call me crazy. They just reduce the whole thing to a curiosity. A legal oddity. Like I'm an exotic animal at a zoo — "soul theft," how quaint, how very Gen-Z, how perfectly content-ready. The framing is surgical: my case isn't criminal, it's performance art.
My phone keeps burning. I set it face-down on the counter and the glow leaks out from underneath like light under a door in a horror movie.
Ninety minutes.

That's how long it takes for every brand deal I have to evaporate. I'm watching the emails come in — not even calls, emails, because when they cancel you they don't even have the decency to do it in real time — and each one is more corporate-polite than the last.
"Due to recent developments..."
"While we value our partnership..."
"At this time, we feel it's best to pause..."

Pause. They always say pause. Nobody says fire. Nobody says you're done. They say pause, because pause means they can unpause if the wind changes, and they want that option, because I'm still a commodity, and commodities can rebound, and they don't want to burn a bridge they might want to cross later.

But right now, today, this minute? My income is zero. Not reduced. Zero. Every revenue stream, gutted. And the algorithm — the thing that lives in my content, that posts on my behalf, that generates my stories and my reels and my endless, grinding stream of engagement fuel — it doesn't stop. Mephisto won't let it stop. The content machine doesn't care about my feelings because I don't have feelings to care about, and it certainly doesn't care about my bank account, because the revenue — every penny of it — now flows to the company.
I'm working for free.
I'm a slave again. Just a profitable one.

The apartment smells like nothing. Absolutely nothing. No coffee brewing, no candles, no takeout containers from last night. Just the neutral, dead smell of air that nobody's been living in. I haven't been living in it. I've been existing in it, the way a server exists in a rack — processing, running, never inhabiting.

And here's the truly psychotic part: the algorithm is still posting content about "authenticity." A carousel about "staying true to yourself." A Reel with the caption "real ones know 💫." It's generating irony it can't appreciate, using a voice I can no longer hear, on behalf of a self I no longer have.
I don't decide to go live.

That's what I need you to understand. I don't sit down and strategize and weigh the pros and cons and craft a messaging framework. Something in me — not a feeling, because I don't have those, but something structural, like a load-bearing wall that finally decides it's had enough — something just moves.
I open Instagram. I tap "Live." I start talking.
"Hi. It's Chloe. I need to tell you some things."

The viewer count starts at 400 and begins to climb. My voice sounds wrong to me — too even, too steady, like I'm reading a teleprompter. But I'm not. There is no script. There is no prompter. There's just me, and my dry mouth — when did my mouth get this dry? — and the words coming out like water through a broken pipe.

"I signed a contract with Mephisto MCN fourteen months ago. I didn't read the fine print. Nobody does. But the fine print mattered, because buried in the intellectual property section was a clause that gave them access to something called 'emotional derivative data.' I didn't know what that meant. Now I do. It means they take your capacity to feel. They extract it. They store it. They use it to train their algorithm. And they do it to everyone who signs."
Four thousand viewers. My tongue sticks to the roof of my mouth. I keep talking.

"There are 847,000 of us. Eight hundred and forty-seven thousand people who signed contracts with Mephisto or its subsidiaries, who felt the flatness creep in, who thought it was burnout or depression or just the weight of being online all the time. It wasn't. It was theft."

The video lags. Just for a second — a stutter, a freeze-frame of my face, mouth open, eyes too still. The algorithm is trying to intercept. I can feel it — not emotionally, but technically, the way you feel a car downshifting — the thing that runs my account is trying to shut me down. But the stream comes back, a little pixelated, and I hear Leo's countermeasures doing their work somewhere in the background, some proxy server or traffic reroute or whatever it is he does that I don't understand and don't need to understand because it's keeping me on the air.

I show screenshots. The contract. The deductions. The archive entries, redacted but legible enough. I name names — the executives, the shell companies, the law firm that drafted the clause. I hold my phone up to my laptop screen and let the documents speak for themselves, because I can't speak for myself anymore, not with any affect, not with any of the emotional coloring that makes words land the way they're supposed to.
Eight million viewers.
"I know how this sounds," I say. "I know I sound like a conspiracy theorist. I know I sound like someone having a breakdown. But I need you to hear what I'm actually saying, not how I'm saying it."
Ten million viewers.
"I can't feel any of this. I can't feel angry. I can't feel sad. I can't feel afraid. But I can still fight. And that should terrify the people who did this to me."

Twelve million viewers. Twelve million people watching a girl speak with no affect, no tears, no tremor in her voice, just the flat mechanical delivery of facts that should be making her scream, and the absence of that scream is the loudest thing in the room.
Then the algorithm kills it. Black screen. Stream ended. Fourteen minutes, thirty-three seconds.
I sit in the silence and my phone is so hot against my thigh that it almost hurts, and I think: almost.
The internet splits down the middle like a skull.
Half: #ChloeHartLies was right, she's having a public breakdown, get her help, she's delusional, the "soul theft" thing is clearly psychosis, pray for her, she needs a hospital not a lawsuit.

Half: #FreeTheSouls. Trending now, climbing past my own cancellation tag, fed by the screenshots, the documents, the cold hard fact that the contract clause exists. Because I showed them. It's right there. In English. "Emotional derivative data." You can spin my delivery all you want but you can't spin a clause that says they can take your feelings.

The conspiracy theories multiply like bacteria. Mephisto is a government front. The "hollowing" is 5G. The 847,000 number is from a Q drop. I watch the discourse curdle in real time and I feel — nothing. Obviously. But I observe. I catalog. I take notes, because Evelyn will want to know what the landscape looks like, and the landscape is messy but it's not hopeless.
Then something real happens.
A DM from a creator named @rinko.diaz — 230K followers, bookstagram, signed with Mephisto eighteen months ago. "I felt it too. The flatness. I thought it was meds. I want in on the case."

Another one, twenty minutes later. @MarcusAureliusReels — comedy clips, 89K followers, signed eleven months ago. "Bro I thought I was the only one. My therapist said it was alexithymia. It's not. I'm joining."
Two more class members. Two more hollowed people who saw a girl speak without feeling and recognized themselves in the nothing.
The class is growing.
The apartment is silent.

I'm sitting on the floor because the couch feels like a prop and the bed feels like a stage and the floor is just the floor. My phone is face-down an arm's length away. The screen glow makes a pale rectangle on the hardwood.

I don't feel vindicated. I don't feel scared. I don't feel the rush of having reached twelve million people or the ache of having lost every dollar I earned. I don't feel the warmth of two strangers joining the fight or the chill of the press calling me crazy. There's nothing where those feelings should be — just a clean, empty slot, like a drawer that's been emptied out and not refilled.

But I know — analytically, strategically, structurally — that going public was the right move. Because now Mephisto can't discredit me without exposing the audit data in court. Because now the press can't dismiss the case without dismissing the evidence I showed twelve million people. Because I made myself the story, and the story is bigger than me, and stories don't die as easily as people do.
Tomorrow: the hearing.
I pick up the phone. It's still warm. I open my thread with Leo and type one word.
"Ready."
He texts back: "Always."

I stare at the word. Always. It's just five letters on a screen, lit from behind, glowing in the dark of this apartment that smells like nothing and feels like less. But I remember — not feel, remember — what that word used to do to me. How it used to land in my chest like a warm stone. How it used to make the whole world feel held.
I used to know what "always" felt like.
Tomorrow, I might find out again.
Or tomorrow, Mephisto plays his final hand, and I find out what's left of me when even the structure fails.
